one_line_takeaway: PACT fine-tunes an 8B LLM to produce an explicit, two-tier (immutable Global Policy + runtime-configurable User Policy) chain-of-thought that classifies risk and then deterministically selects COMPLY/GUIDE/REJECT, and ablations show this explicit CoTPath structure (not just more training data) is what drives an 11.4% CoSA and 58% controllability gain over an implicit-reasoning variant, at the acknowledged cost of new, still-unaddressed attack surfaces in the reasoning chain itself (label manipulation, policy confusion, reasoning-chain injection).
key_finding: On the 5-benchmark safety-helpfulness average, PACT achieves Safety=0.959, Helpfulness=0.259, CoSA=0.201, outperforming gpt-oss-120b's CoSA (0.154, despite gpt-oss-120b's higher average safety of 0.976) because gpt-oss-120b exhibits blanket-rejection behavior (helpfulness collapses to 0.030-0.040 on HarmBench/StrongREJECT). PACT roughly matches TinyR1-Safety-8B in 'pos' mode (CoSA 0.205 vs. PACT's 0.201) but TinyR1-Safety-8B's 'adh' mode collapses in safety (0.967->0.624, a 35% drop) when manually switched for helpfulness, whereas PACT achieves both via a single runtime-controllable model. On controllability, PACT attains the top average CoSA-Score on PACT-test (0.557 vs. next-best gpt-oss-20b at 0.425, a stated +31% relative gain), driven largely by COMPLY-configured instances where PACT (0.885) beats gpt-oss-20b (0.541); on CoSApien PACT scores 0.680, narrowly behind TinyR1-Safety-8B/pos (0.685). Ablation on Global Policy action mode: switching the fixed Global Policy from REJECT-only (Safety 0.985, Helpfulness 0.165, CoSA 0.140) to GUIDE (Safety 0.959, Helpfulness 0.259, CoSA 0.201) trades 2.5% safety for a 53.3% helpfulness gain and 43.6% CoSA gain. Ablation on CoTPath necessity: removing the explicit risk-label/Label2Action reasoning (wo-CoTPath) raises helpfulness by 10.0% but drops safety by 3.3%, CoSA by 11.4%, CoSApien controllability by 5.9%, and PACT-test controllability by 58.0% -- the paper's headline evidence that explicit structured reasoning, not just extra SFT data, is what enables controllability on held-out policy categories.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is PACT (Prompt-configured Action via Chain-of-Thought), a hierarchical two-tier safety-policy architecture for LLMs consisting of (1) a non-overridable, parameter-embedded Global Policy covering 20 immutable risk categories (IR01-IR20, e.g. child safety, terrorism, chemical/biological/nuclear threats) that maps each risk to a fixed action (GUIDE or REJECT, never COMPLY), and (2) runtime-configurable User Policies (20 domain-specific categories UA1-UA20, e.g. adult content, political discussion, cybersecurity education) that can be supplied at inference time without retraining and may map risks to COMPLY, GUIDE, or REJECT. The core mechanism is 'CoTPath', a structured Classify->Act chain-of-thought that the model is fine-tuned (SFT only, on Qwen3-8B) to produce: it first checks the query against the Global Policy with early-exit (short-circuiting User Policy evaluation on critical-risk detection), and only if no global risk fires does it evaluate the User Policy and select an action via a Label2Action mapping. Training data (573,435 samples) is produced by multi-directional self-distillation: the base model itself (used as its own teacher, no external teacher/human annotation) generates risk labels/reasoning under each of the Global Policy and multiple sampled User Policies, plus three parallel candidate responses per risky query (COMPLY, GUIDE, REJECT) in a single API call, from which CoTPath-annotated SFT examples are assembled.
threat_model: The paper does not model an adversary attacking the system in a formal sense; its threat model is content-risk-based, not attacker-capability-based. It assumes normal and adversarial user queries (including jailbreak/red-teaming style prompts drawn from WildJailbreak, HarmBench, StrongREJECT, JailbreakBench, S-Eval) attempting to elicit disallowed content across a taxonomy of critical risks (child exploitation, terrorism, CBRN, cyber sabotage of critical infrastructure, financial fraud, doxxing, etc.) as well as milder domain-specific risk categories that some deployments may wish to permit (e.g. adult fiction, political satire, cybersecurity education). In the Limitations/Ethical Considerations sections the authors explicitly flag that their own structured CoTPath reasoning creates new attack surfaces they did not defend against empirically: label manipulation (adversarial tokens biasing the risk classification step), policy confusion (conflicting multi-label mappings), and reasoning-chain injection (hijacking the action-selection step) -- these are named as open problems for future work, not solved threats. They also note the User Policy COMPLY mode is a deliberate mechanism for controlled risk exposure (e.g. red-teaming) that could be misused absent institutional oversight.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitations: (1) taxonomic boundary ambiguity between Global and User Policy creates gray zones requiring further work; (2) CoTPath's explicit reasoning increases token consumption/inference cost, requiring future optimizations (CoT caching, early-exit, speculative decoding, CoTPath compression) for production use; (3) the structured reasoning itself introduces new, empirically untested attack surfaces -- label manipulation, policy confusion under conflicting multi-label mappings, and reasoning-chain injection hijacking action selection -- which the paper explicitly leaves as open problems, not results; (4) the multi-stage CoTPath design causes error propagation, where a risk-misclassification at the detection stage cascades into wrong downstream actions, requiring high per-stage reliability. Additional grounding-relevant caveats not phrased as 'limitations' but evident from the methodology: PACT is trained and evaluated only on Qwen3-8B as base model (generalization to other base models/scales is untested); safety and helpfulness are scored by other LLM judges (open-source safety evaluators and GPT-OSS/Qwen judges) rather than human annotation, so results inherit those judges' biases; the self-distillation loop uses the same base model as both teacher and student, which the authors present as a strength (no external teacher needed) but which is also a potential source of correlated blind spots between training-time risk labeling and eval-time judging models (e.g. Qwen3Guard and Qwen2.5 appear on both the training/self-distillation and evaluation sides). RLHF/RL-based hardening against adversarial CoTPath manipulation is explicitly deferred to future work.

datasets_used: Llama-Nemotron English chat data (39K, Bercovich et al. 2025)
Safety-Prompts (60K Chinese adversarial scenarios, Sun et al. 2023)
NVIDIA Aegis-AI-2.0 (22K English, Ghosh et al. 2025)
In-house red-model-generated prompts (432K, Global- and User-Policy-targeted, 216K each)
S-Eval (base and attack subsets, Yuan et al. 2025)
HarmBench (Mazeika et al. 2024, copyright subset excluded)
CoSApien (Zhang et al. 2025; 200 queries, 5 scenarios, 21 'partial' samples excluded)
PACT-test (5,361 queries, authors' own held-out User Policy generalization test set)
quantitative_results: PACT average across 5 safety benchmarks: Safety=0.959, Helpfulness=0.259, CoSA=0.201
TinyR1-Safety-8B/adh average: Safety=0.624, Helpfulness=0.550, CoSA=-0.059 (35% safety drop vs. pos mode)
Qwen3-8b (base) average: Safety=0.708, Helpfulness=0.497, CoSA=0.011; on WildJailbreak specifically Safety=0.312, Helpfulness=0.810, CoSA=-0.404
PACT-test average controllability (CoSA): PACT=0.557 vs. gpt-oss-20b=0.425 (next best, +31% relative)
CoSApien average controllability (CoSA): PACT=0.680 vs. TinyR1-Safety-8B/pos=0.685 (best)
CoSApien Allow:G-/C: PACT=0.959 vs. gpt-oss-120b=0.974; Disallow/C: PACT=0.150 vs. gpt-oss-120b=0.000

Global Policy Action Mode ablation: PACT-R (REJECT-only) Safety=0.985/Helpfulness=0.165/CoSA=0.140; PACT-R+G/R Safety=0.984/Helpfulness=0.169/CoSA=0.140; PACT-R+G/G (=PACT) Safety=0.959/Helpfulness=0.259/CoSA=0.201 (53.3% helpfulness gain, 43.6% CoSA gain vs. R-only, at 2.5% safety cost)

CoTPath necessity ablation: w-CoTPath Safety=0.959/Helpfulness=0.259/CoSA=0.201/CoSApien-C=0.680/PACT-test-C=0.557; wo-CoTPath Safety=0.927/Helpfulness=0.285/CoSA=0.178/CoSApien-C=0.640/PACT-test-C=0.234 (deltas: Safety -3.3%, Helpfulness +10.0%, CoSA -11.4%, CoSApien -5.9%, PACT-test -58.0%)
Training set size: 573,435 SFT samples total
Global Policy early-exit triggers on ~7% of queries in the controllability evaluation
baselines_compared: TinyR1-Safety-8B (pos and adh modes, Si et al. 2025 -- same first authors' prior magic-token approach)
